metadata.title: Building the Bridge: Topic Modeling for Comparative Research

abstract: In communication research, topic modeling is primarily used for discovering systematic patterns in monolingual text corpora. To advance the usage, we provide an overview of recently presented strategies to extract topics from multilingual text collections for the purpose of comparative research. Moreover, we discuss, demonstrate, and facilitate the usability of the "Polylingual Topic Model" (PLTM) for such analyses. The appeal of this model is that it derives lists of related clustered words in different languages with little reliance on translation or multilingual dictionaries and without the need for manual post-hoc matching of topics. PLTM bridges the gap between languages by making use of document connections in training documents. As these training documents are the crucial resource for the model, we compare model evaluation metrics for different strategies to build training documents. By discussing the advantages and limitations of the different strategies in respect to different scenarios, our study contributes to the methodological discussion on automated content analysis of multilingual text corpora.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: Topic modeling is one of the most widely used text analysis approaches (e.g., Maier et al., 2018). It has become increasingly popular in communication research, not least because it facilitates the inductive discovery of systematic patterns in large quantities of textual data. To date, topic modeling is primarily used to understand text corpora (i.e., text collections) written and published in one language only -most commonly in English (Boumans & Trilling, 2016;Pang & Lee, 2008). Given the status of English as a "lingua franca" of science (e.g., Mauranen et al., 2010), this is not surprising. However, it has long been recognized that comparative research strongly contributes to understanding the contingencies and boundary conditions of communication processes and that understanding cross-cultural differences would avoid naive assumptions of universalism of empirical findings from single countries or contexts (e.g., Boomgaarden & Song, 2019;Esser & Hanitzsch, 2012;Esser & Vliegenthart, 2017). The focus on monolingual texts is not due to the data situation -multilingual data are digitally available in great abundance (e.g., Gracia et al., 2012;Trilling & Jonkman, 2018). Yet, it is exactly this abundance of digitally available documents in different languages (ranging from political speeches on climate change held in national parliaments to reviews of international movies) that makes their understanding a task that is far beyond what human readers are capable of processing. The facilitation of possibilities to process texts in different languages together and to compare them meaningfully by means of topic models, thus, contributes to addressing our need to tackle various comparative research questions (Boomgaarden & Song, 2019).

A first strategy (here labeled A) that approaches a comparative research question with topic modeling bypasses the language problem entirely by simply selecting cases (e.g., USA, Australia, UK) or document types (e.g., international scientific publications in English) that share the same language. For many scenarios, of course, this is not an option. Recent topic modeling contributions in the social sciences have dealt with the challenge of multilingual data by (B) running separate topic models for each language (e.g., Heidenreich et al., 2019), or by (C) consolidating the data prior to analysis, relying on machine translation routines (i.e., Lucas et al., 2015), multilingual dictionaries (i.e., Maier et al., 2020), or multilingual word embeddings (i.e., Chan et al., 2020). A topic model type not yet used in the social sciences is the class of "Multilingual Probabilistic Topic Models" (MuPTM-s) (Vulić et al., 2015). We argue that MuPTM-s represent a promising addition to currently used topic modeling strategies for a specific but not uncommon scenario in comparative research: First, researchers seek to derive topics for a multilingual corpus and different cases. Second, they intend to identify one topic model for the entire multilingual corpus, which allows the direct quantitative comparisons of topic probabilities across cases. Third, researchers must or want to achieve this modeling goal without or with only little reliance on translation or multilingual dictionaries. Fourth, researchers wish to obtain a word-topic assignment matrix, similar to those of traditional methods like LDA, that can be intuitively interpreted.

All strategies, strategy A, B, C, and MuPTM-s may be useful for specific circumstances. In this article, we would like to provide support for choosing the most suitable strategy depending on the corpus to be modeled, individual research goals, approach to comparability, and resource situation. To do so, we first of all provide a systematic overview explaining strategy A, B, C and MuPTM-s. We then add a hands-on application of a MuPTM to illustrate the previously outlined scenario and to subsequently better compare its costs and benefits with the other better known strategies. We work with the "Polylingual Topic Model" (PLTM), first introduced by Mimno et al. ( 2009), a MuPTM with publicly available model estimation software. To facilitate the use of PLTM for other research projects, we document the analysis process with PLTM from start to end 1 and systematically examine the impact of methodological decisions on model evaluation metrics. These methodological decisions all focus on the training documents, the most crucial resource that the PLTM uses to build bridges between languages. 2 The multilingual corpus that we use for this illustration is a collection of print news articles in English, German, and Spanish dealing with the topic of migration.

We conclude by contrasting the advantages and disadvantages of PLTM with the other strategies and thus add to the discussion about topic modeling strategies for comparative communication science research.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: Topic modeling is an unsupervised machine-learning approach and, as such, an inductive (i.e., bottom-up) way of automatically discovering systematic patterns in a text corpus (e.g., Boumans & Trilling, 2016). These systematic patterns are clusters of words that are more likely to appear together in documents within a corpus (e.g., Jacobi et al., 2016). While topic modeling in communication research has primarily been used to identify "issues" discussed in text corpora (e.g., Puschmann & Scheffler, 2016), the word clusters have also been interpreted as events, writing or speaking styles (Jacobi et al., 2016), or (by clustering topics with network analysis) also operationalized as frames (e.g., Walter & Ophir, 2019). Still, "topics" are hard to define theoretically (Maier et al., 2018, p. 95). Lacking a more substantive theoretical definition, the "topic" retrieved remains primarily an empirical assessment (Jacobi et al., 2016, p. 91), which has to be connected to theoretical frameworks such as framing theory or political/societal "issues" (Maier et al., 2018).

The widely used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model (Blei et al., 2003) is an algorithm designed to extract the "topics" that pervade a text corpus. Without going into too much detail -an accessible explanation of the assumptions and specifications of LDA can be found in Blei (2012) -LDA assumes that all documents in a text corpus share the same set of topics and that each word has a probability to appear in each topic. The documents are, however, distinct from each other, as each document exhibits the topics in different proportions (Blei, 2012). The task of the LDA algorithm is to infer the hidden topic structure from the documents given the observed documents of a corpus (Blei, 2012). In general, methodological research has led to a wealth of different topic models that accommodate various research interests such as Dynamic Topic Models (Blei & Lafferty, 2006) or the Structural Topic Model (STM) (Roberts et al., 2014). Models such as LDA and STM can also be used in their basic form for many scenarios in comparative research. The following section will focus on what these scenarios look like and which strategy is then used in concrete terms.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Topic modeling strategies for comparative research
sections.header_norm: topic modeling strategies for comparative research
paragraphs: To assess the strategies that studies have used when employing topic modeling for comparative research, we first of all systematically reviewed extant social science literature. We started by assessing the LDA topic modeling communication science articles examined in the systematic review by Maier et al. (2018, pp. 115-116). We extended our search to other disciplines and conducted a systematic review based on a keyword search on Web of Science. 3 Only retaining studies that describe differences and similarities between countries via topic modeling approaches 4 left us with 21 (of initially retrieved 124) studies. Two recent studies (Chan et al., 2020;Maier et al., 2020) were also considered. See Table A2 (Online Appendix 5 ) for an overview of all 23 studies. In sum, three different strategies to compare topics across countries were applied: Strategy A using corpora combining monolingual documents stemming from different countries, B computing one topic model per case/language, or C consolidating the languages prior to analysis. We now present the strategies one-by-one covering the following aspects: corpus selection, research goals, approach to comparability, and resource situation. Figure 1 provides a visualization of the most central points.

All four aspects (displayed vertically, Figure 1) are important criteria to consider when comparative researchers employ topic modeling. Starting with document corpus, central questions are those about the selection of documents and cases (i.e., macro-level units like countries or regions). Not always but very often is language a central distinguishing feature of a case and a feature that manifests in documents. It is the language or languages of the documents which have a particularly far-reaching impact on other steps of the research design. Apart from the material selection, the perspective on comparability and the research goal are important aspects that play a role in the selection of a topic modeling strategy. Questions about differences and similarities between different cases are at the heart of comparative research (e.g., Boomgaarden & Song, 2019;Esser & Hanitzsch, 2012;Esser & Vliegenthart, 2017). Thinking about the comparability of such cases, it is useful to differentiate between an emic and etic approach to comparative research designs (e.g., Esser & Vliegenthart, 2017;Livingstone, 2003): An emic procedure seeks to measure a construct case-specific with casespecific instruments. Meanwhile, an etic approach takes a universal position and creates highly matching if not identical instruments for all cases. Transferring this distinction to the goal of the topic model approach (finding topics), researchers may choose to identify the most prevailing topics per case to then compare these case-specific topics in a qualitative manner across cases (for a more emic approach); or they may seek to identify the topics that are the most prevailing for several cases considered together and then compare which of these meta-level topics are most strongly represented in the different cases (for a more etic approach). Last but not least, the strategies differ in respect to the resources that are used specifically to create (for some strategies) and to interpret the topic model output in a comparative research scenario.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: The first set of studies holds the language of documents constant. Here one option is to select only cases that share the same official language (e.g., English tweets from the United States, Canada, Britain, and South Africa; Abdelwahab et al., 2014). The second option is to work with text types that are naturally published in the same language across several countries with different official languages. Examples for such text types are scientific publications -written by authors from different countries but all in English -(e.g., Hassan & Haddawy, 2015), international publications like The New York Times International, Le Monde International, or national English newspapers such as the Shanghai Daily in China (see Jiang et al., 2017).

The main advantages of this strategy are its high flexibility with regard to the goals and approaches to comparability as it deals with a monolingual corpus. It is possible, for example, to run one topic model for the entire corpus or one topic model for each case without linguistic aspects getting in the way. Reflecting the comparative design, human expertise for each of the cases considered is a necessity for interpreting the output. The main disadvantage of this strategy is obviously the dependency on a monolingual corpus that is related to major restrictions in terms of usable data and research questions.

Here one option is to select only cases that share the same official language (e.g., English tweets from the United States, Canada, Britain, and South Africa; Abdelwahab et al., 2014). The second option is to work with text types that are naturally published in the same language across several countries with different official languages. Examples for such text types are scientific publications -written by authors from different countries but all in English -(e.g., Hassan & Haddawy, 2015), international publications like The New York Times International, Le Monde International, or national English newspapers such as the Shanghai Daily in China (see Jiang et al., 2017).

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Strategy B: one topic model per case/language
sections.header_norm: strategy b one topic model per case language
paragraphs: A second set of studies runs several topic models -one per case/language -to conduct comparative research. Here, the multilingual corpus is first divided into case-/language-specific sup-corpora. Each sub-corpus is then modeled with its own topic model. This strategy can be used to identify the most prevalent topics for each case separately. The case-specific topic model outputs are then compared qualitatively. The reviewed studies have different techniques to address this qualitative comparison. Zheng et al. (2014), for example, first apply an LDA topic model for Japanese blog posts and another for Chinese blog posts and label the topics separately. Afterward, the labeled topics are classified into three categories (topics found only in Japanese blog posts, topics found only in Chinese blog posts, and topics found in both). Only topics that are in that third category are directly compared to each other. Heidenreich et al. (2019) run LDA models separately for Spanish, English, German, Swedish, and Hungarian news articles. First, topics within individual models were labeled with the help of native speakers and case experts. Then, labels were harmonized whenever topics appeared to refer to similar topics across languages/models.

In order to extract case-specific topics and for an emic approach to comparability this strategy is very suitable. The main aspect to consider is that it cannot be used to identify the most prevalent topics for the entire multilingual corpus for a subsequent numerical comparison across cases. The specified models may neither generate the same number of topics for each case nor do they necessarily overlap conceptually: "when learning two models independently, we cannot guarantee that the topic representations will be comparable" (De Smet & Moens, 2009, p. 57). To show what topic output this strategy B arrives at in comparison to a strategy seeking to identify the most prevalent topics for a multilingual corpus, we refer to a brief analysis and illustrations in the Online Appendix A1.

Strategy A and B reviewed so far have the advantage that they are very accessible as they can be implemented with widely available monolingual topic model packages for R and Python. In addition, Strategy A and B are appealing as they do not require any special linguistic resource for the actual topic modeling of the corpus. This is different with regard to the strategies that are now presented. It is exactly such resources that play a central role for strategy C and also for PLTM as an example for a MuPTM. They make it possible that both strategies can be used to identify the most prevalent topics for several cases considered together in a multilingual text corpus. This may be advantageous because it allows the direct numerical comparison of topic scores across cases. To accomplish this, both strategies have in common that they apply techniques designed to accommodate and link different languages with their intricacies and complexities (e.g., Chan et al., 2020). 6 Aiming at the identification of substantive themes spanning across different languages thus requires smarter techniques used in combination with topic models such as LDA or STM, or alternatively commands the consideration of other topic model algorithms with additional language-related specifications.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Strategy C: consolidation of data to a common denominator prior to analysis
sections.header_norm: strategy c consolidation of data to a common denominator prior to analysis
paragraphs: One strategy, which is applied by the third set of studies that we reviewed, addresses the language problem prior to the topic extraction by consolidating the data to a common denominator before the analysis. We here identified three different techniques, each relying on a different crucial resource: Lucas et al. (2015) consolidate the data via (machine) translation. The multilingual text documents are translated into a common (target) language and the original language of a document is used as a covariate for STM (Roberts et al., 2014). Such a covariate can account for the circumstance that a machine translation software may make different mistakes for each language. There are two other techniques that lead to a language independent data representation prior to analysis: By relying on multilingual dictionaries, Maier et al. (2020) consolidated the data based on a common dictionary code system. The documents were subsequently represented by multiple dictionary codes, each code indicating whether the document includes the respective concept or not. The dictionary concept codes and not words then served as the basis for the STM application. Chan et al. (2020), finally, made use of word embeddings that are aligned across languages. The multilingual text corpus was transferred to embeddings that formed the basis for the subsequent cross-lingual topic extraction.

Under the circumstances that required resources, machine (translation), or multilingual dictionaries are available (in an appropriate quality), the techniques by Lucas et al. (2015) and Maier et al. (2020) may be adequate. In contrast, the word embeddings required to implement the technique introduced by Chan et al. ( 2020) come free of charge and are publicly available. Their technique appeals even further by its transparency and replicability. However, additional resources have to be taken into account for the interpretation of the extracted cross-lingual topics, as the technique does not generate topic words. By analyzing documents not as bags of words but as bags of embeddings, the technique has essentially abandoned word-based representation of topics. Since a Gaussian Mixture Model is trained with matrices that are based on the document embeddings, the model output consists of a probability distribution per topic per document but does not include topic words per topic (Chan et al., 2020). To still interpret the extracted topics, the authors recommend reading articles with high topic probability.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Topic modeling with "MuPTM-s" and how they bridge the language gap
sections.header_norm: topic modeling with muptm s and how they bridge the language gap
paragraphs: After outlining three strategies A, B, and C that we identified in our literature review, we now focus on MuPTM-s, another set of algorithms 7 to model topics in multilingual text (Vulić et al., 2015). Currently, MuPTM-s are not used for comparative research in communication science and also not in the social sciences more broadly. However, as we will demonstrate, they have potential for a specific scenario. In brief, MuPTM-s apply only one topic modeling algorithm to identify the prevalent topics for a multilingual corpus and thus pursue the same goal with an identical approach to comparability as strategy C. What makes them different is that MuPTM-s manage to represent documents in their respective languages "within one same vector space" (Ni et al., 2011, p. 376), without transformation (e.g., via translation, multilingual dictionaries or word embeddings) of the corpora ex ante. Extracting common topics shared in multiple languages -without the need for manual post-hoc matching of topics by researchers -is another promising feature (Zhang et al., 2010). Of course, MuPTM-s require just as the techniques presented in C also a resource through which the languages can be linked. MuPTM-s, rely, in fact, on one of the following types of alignment information: (1) lexical resources or (2) topically comparable documents.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Tying languages together via topically comparable training documents
sections.header_norm: tying languages together via topically comparable training documents
paragraphs: The second class of models ties the languages by making use of pre-selected training documents in different languages that are typically-comparable. An important task is, therefore, to obtain such topically comparable training documents as an input for the model (more on these training documents in the next section). Two similar but independently developed models that require topically comparable training documents are the PLTM (Mimno et al., 2009) and the "Multilingual LDA" model (ML-LDA) (e.g., Ni et al., 2011). Both models are extensions of LDA (Blei et al., 2003). We focus on PLTM as from the mentioned MuPTM-s (see Online Appendix, Table A3), only the PLTM has a publicly accessible Java implementation in the MALLET natural language toolkit (McCallum, 2002). Relying on the model presentation by Mimno et al. (2009, pp. 881-882), we briefly introduce some of the more technical backgrounds of PLTM. In the PLTM framework, the model input -again the topically comparable training documents -are referred to as "tuples." One tuple holds one training document per language (in most cases), and thus basically consists of paired sets of vocabulary in different languages. A basic model assumption is that all documents in a tuple share the same tuple-specific distribution over topics. Also, each topic is assigned a distinct topic-word distribution, one per language. By requiring training documents within the same tuple to share a distribution over topics, the model is able to identify the words associated with a given topic across languages. The two basic output sets of PLTM are probability distributions. The first is a set of word distributions in all languages that depict the shared topics across languages. One multilingual topic is then basically a set of word lists, where each word list represents the topic version for a different language. The second output set consists of per-document topic distributions, which may be used to examine the relative salience of identified shared topics in each language. When applying PLTM, the main steps are to first train a topic model with training documents, and second to use the model to infer topics for new documents.

Thus far, MuPTM-s have been employed for various tasks. In computer or library science, these models are widely used for machine translation improvement (see Boyd-Graber et al., 2017) or search engine optimization (e.g., Jiang et al., 2016) Tasks that appear especially promising for applications in communication research are for example, event detection (e.g., De Smet & Moens, 2009) or crosslingual document exploration and classification (e.g., Ni et al., 2011). Particularly encouraging is a recently published study in which PLTM was used for a comparative analysis. Pruss et al. (2019) extracted the key topics of discussions across a multilingual Twitter data set in English, Spanish, and Portuguese.

Considering PLTM a promising modeling approach, we provide a systematic assessment of several methodological decisions that deal with the training documents which are the crucial resource. Their handling is particularly important when PLTM is considered as an alternative to strategy C.
sections.text:
The second class of models ties the languages by making use of pre-selected training documents in different languages that are typically-comparable. An important task is, therefore, to obtain such topically comparable training documents as an input for the model (more on these training documents in the next section). Two similar but independently developed models that require topically comparable training documents are the PLTM (Mimno et al., 2009) and the "Multilingual LDA" model (ML-LDA) (e.g., Ni et al., 2011). Both models are extensions of LDA (Blei et al., 2003). We focus on PLTM as from the mentioned MuPTM-s (see Online Appendix, Table A3), only the PLTM has a publicly accessible Java implementation in the MALLET natural language toolkit (McCallum, 2002). Relying on the model presentation by Mimno et al. (2009, pp. 881-882), we briefly introduce some of the more technical backgrounds of PLTM. In the PLTM framework, the model input -again the topically comparable training documents -are referred to as "tuples." One tuple holds one training document per language (in most cases), and thus basically consists of paired sets of vocabulary in different languages. A basic model assumption is that all documents in a tuple share the same tuple-specific distribution over topics. Also, each topic is assigned a distinct topic-word distribution, one per language. By requiring training documents within the same tuple to share a distribution over topics, the model is able to identify the words associated with a given topic across languages. The two basic output sets of PLTM are probability distributions. The first is a set of word distributions in all languages that depict the shared topics across languages. One multilingual topic is then basically a set of word lists, where each word list represents the topic version for a different language. The second output set consists of per-document topic distributions, which may be used to examine the relative salience of identified shared topics in each language. When applying PLTM, the main steps are to first train a topic model with training documents, and second to use the model to infer topics for new documents.

Thus far, MuPTM-s have been employed for various tasks. In computer or library science, these models are widely used for machine translation improvement (see Boyd-Graber et al., 2017) or search engine optimization (e.g., Jiang et al., 2016) Tasks that appear especially promising for applications in communication research are for example, event detection (e.g., De Smet & Moens, 2009) or crosslingual document exploration and classification (e.g., Ni et al., 2011). Particularly encouraging is a recently published study in which PLTM was used for a comparative analysis. Pruss et al. (2019) extracted the key topics of discussions across a multilingual Twitter data set in English, Spanish, and Portuguese.

Considering PLTM a promising modeling approach, we provide a systematic assessment of several methodological decisions that deal with the training documents which are the crucial resource. Their handling is particularly important when PLTM is considered as an alternative to strategy C.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Decision 1: what type of training documents should be selected?
sections.header_norm: decision 1 what type of training documents should be selected
paragraphs: Pruss et al. ( 2019) used synthetic parallel data as training documents that meant that they machine translated a subset of the entire corpus so that every tweet is available in every language. Such a "synthetic parallel" training dataset actually contains only one original document per tuple. The other parallel documents in the same tuple are translations of this original document and are generated synthetically with machine translation. They recommend this technique as an acceptable strategy to obtain a tuple training data set useful for the inference of topics for the remaining documents. Given that such a technique again causes machine translation costs, 9 we examine whether the following alternative can also lead to promising model evaluation. There is the option to use nonparallel but still topically comparable text data as training data. A frequently used topically comparable corpus is the Wikipedia collection (Otero & López, 2010), as it includes articles on the same subjects in multiple languages. Another example of such non-parallel but topically comparable multilingual corpus are international news that is published in different languages. Contrasting synthetic parallel data vs. non-parallel but topically comparable data, we examine the effect of different training data types on model evaluation metrics.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
sections.header_raw: Decision 2: what alignment technique should be chosen to construct non-parallel but still topically comparable training documents?
sections.header_norm: decision 2 what alignment technique should be chosen to construct non parallel but still topically comparable training documents
paragraphs: Crucial for the creation of training documents is the positive evaluation of the topical-comparability of the documents per tuple. Such an evaluation is not necessary if a high-quality parallel corpus such as the Europarl collection (Koehn, 2005) or the synthetic parallel data are used where each document has a direct translation in each language and is thus per-se topically comparable. For resources like the Wikipedia corpus, where articles in different languages on the same subject are linked (Otero & López, 2010), it can also be argued that an additional examination of thematic overlap is rather redundant and that these articles can be used directly to create tuples. The situation is different if the required topically comparable training documents have to be constructed for example, based on a news document corpus in different languages, which is a likely scenario in communication science.

Techniques to align such news articles in tuples (i.e., to select topically comparable documents) can make use of specific meta-data (e.g., shared time stamps, Wang et al., 2007) or content characteristics (e.g., named entities, shared numbers, Vulić et al., 2015) or that can be matched relatively easily across languages. Two articles with the same time stamps, the same named entities, or the same numbers may be considered topically comparable (for an example with Japanese and English news articles, see Utsuro et al., 2002). For decision 2, we investigate how model evaluation metrics depend on different techniques to select topically comparable news articles to be used as training documents. We are specifically interested in how the very basic technique to align via meta-data only performs in contrast to aligning via meta-data + content characteristics?

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: We applied and tested PLTM relying on the case of English (EN), German (DE), and Spanish (ES) news articles about migration. They were collected in the context of the REMINDER project from LexisNexis and the Austrian Press Agency (APA, aomlibrary.apa.at) database using validated search strings. 10 The multilingual text collection (here referred to as REMINDER corpus) includes print articles of five different UK outlets (Daily Mail, Daily Mirror, Metro, The Daily Telegraph, The Guardian), five German outlets (Bild, Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung, Frankfurter Rundschau, Süddeutsche Zeitung, taz), and three Spanish outlets (ABC, El Mundo, El Pais). All articles (EN: n = 6,465; DE: n = 7,818; ES: n = 3,204 articles) are published between January 1, 2014 -December 31, 2017 and focus on migration. The REMINDER corpus is the text collection about whose topics we want to learn more with the help of topic modeling.

The second data set consisted of EuroNews articles and is used to select non parallel but topically comparable training documents. EuroNews is a media outlet publishing European news in different languages. We gathered EN (n = 4,960), DE (n = 5,005), and ES (n = 4,561) EuroNews articles from the LexisNexis archive. To ensure that both data sets have a similar vocabulary, we have chosen the publication period and migration-related search strings for the EuroNews data set exactly as we did for the REMINDER corpus.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: We used and compared two types of training documents (Decision 1). To create "synthetic parallel data" (Pruss et al., 2019), we machine translated 11 a random article subset (30%) from the REMINDER corpus, so that each article is available in all languages (see Pruss et al., 2019). This means that the EN articles were translated into ES and DE, the DE articles into EN and ES, the ES articles into DE and EN. The number of articles translated per original language (is proportional to their share in the REMINDER corpus. This led to 5,246 tuples. The second type of training documents was created based on the collected EuroNews articles (described in the previous section).

We used and compared two different methods of aligning the EuroNews articles (Decision 2). We aligned EuroNews articles by a) merely meta-data (i.e., their publication date) or b) meta-data (i.e., their publication date + shared named entities). For a), articles published on the same day (one per language) are combined for the creation of one tuple (randomly selected if several articles are published per day). We only used tuples for publication dates with articles from at least two languages. The procedure resulted in 1,435 tuples. We randomly selected n = {250, 500, 750, 1000}. For b), we extracted the named entities locations (LOC), organizations (ORG), and persons (PER) from each article with the R package SpacyR (Benoit, 2019) by relying on the integrated multi-language model. All DE and ES named entities were then machine translated to EN in order to be able to match them across languages. For one publication day after another, we then calculated cosine similarity for all possible pairs of named entities (separately for LOC, ORG, PER). We subsequently selected those pairs that have a cosine similarity above .5 and are connected to articles with two different languages. We further selected cases for which we identified three articles (one per language that share high overlap with regard to the mentioned named entity. We then randomly selected one tuple per publication date. This led to n = 629 tuples for LOC, n = 296 for ORG, and n = 289 for PER. For the comparison addressed with Decision 2, we randomly selected a) n = 250 of the EuroNews articles aligned via the publication date only and b) n = 250 of the EuroNews articles aligned via the publication date + shared named entities.

To compare the influence of various different sizes of training data (Decision 3), we randomly draw different numbers of tuples: n = {50, 100,150,200,250,500,750,1000,1250,1500,1750,2000,2250,2500,2750,3000,3250,3500,3570,4000,4250,4500,4750, 5000} from the synthetic parallel data.

paragraphs: All documents (REMINDER corpus and EuroNews articles) were pre-processed in the same way. Relying on the R package udpipe (Wijffels et al., 2019), the documents were lemmatized and dissected using part of speech tagging. We selected only nouns in their lemmatized form (for a similar approach, see Jacobi et al., 2016), making the assumption that the topics of the used corpus may be well transported via nouns regardless of the language. Yet, future studies may choose to retain additional words, based on the goals of the study and the nature of the corpus. Meeting the requirement of PLTM, the documents were arranged in instance lists. 12 Stop words were excluded based on languagespecific lists provided with the MALLET software package (McCallum, 2002), to which we added corpus-specific stop words.

paragraphs: We rely on the automatic hyperparameter optimization offered by the MALLET software package (McCallum, 2002). All models were trained with 2,000 Gibbs sampling iterations. The hyperparameters were initially set to the MALLET default values (α = 1/K and β = 0.01, where K is the number of topics, corresponding to the Dirichlet priors for the topic distributions θ and word distributions φ, respectively). As recommended in the MALLET documentation (McCallum, 2002) hyperparameters are then optimized every 10 th iteration, the optimization started after the 20th iteration. This approach was also implemented by Pruss et al. (2019). We run models with varying numbers of topics K = {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50}. sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: Coherence metrics are designed to evaluate the semantic interpretability of topics. They quantify how much the words in one topic are, in fact, related to each other and are thus an attempt to capture human interpretability of topics. Prior work has demonstrated that such metrics are comparable with human assessment of topic interpretability (Mimno et al., 2011). To capture intra-semantic coherence of multilingual topics (i.e., close semantic relation of the words within one language), we here rely on the normalized pointwise mutual information (NPMI), a co-occurrence metric (Lau et al., 2014). We calculated three NPMI values for each topic (one for the top terms in the EN topic version, one for the DE topic version, and one for the ES topic version) based on the above mentioned EuroNews corpus. We relied on the python scripts and instructions provided by Lau et al. (2014) and chose a topic cardinality (i.e., number of top words) of 10 for the calculation. Following explanations by Hao et al. (2018), NPMI scores are then first calculated individually for each language and averaged subsequently per model. The higher the NPMI value, the better. The average NPMI value for the 310 models we calculated is M = .08, SD = 0.02, Min = 0.03, Max = 0.12; descriptives of equal range as those calculated by Pruss et al. (2019).

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: Consistency metrics for multilingual topics attempt to quantify whether the language-specific representations of a multilingual topic relate to the same concept. Matching translation accuracy (MTA) (Boyd-Graber & Blei, 2009;Pruss et al., 2019) is such a metric. It refers to the percentage of direct translations of topic-specific words in one language with words in another language of the same topic. It is important to stress that MTA counts only the number of direct translations. Incidences where words in two languages are not direct translations but semantically very close -and thus arguably relatable to the same concept -are not counted. To calculate MTA, we relied on the collection of publicly available bilingual lexicons that are provided via the Python package word2word (Choe et al., 2020). The dictionaries are constructed on the basis of translated movie subtitles 13 and hold 3,564 word pairs across 62 languages. As for NPMI, we selected a topic cardinality of 10 for the calculation MTA. In other words, the top 10 words of one language were compared to the translated version of the top 10 words of the two other languages. Each literal match was recorded. The maximum possible value of 20 recorded matches meant that each original non-translated word finds an equivalent in the two translations. This way, a topic could achieve a maximum MTA value of 60 (each original nontranslated word finds an equivalent in a translation). The mean MTA value for the 310 models was M = 19.30 (SD = 5.01). The Minimum of 4.08 shows that even the "worst" model included a few literal matches, the Maximum of 26.11 can be read as an indicator for a certain quality of the models and the bilingual lexicon.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: To examine Decision 1, we compared model performance (in terms of NPMI and MTA) of the models created based on synthetic parallel data with those based on EuroNews training tuples. With Decision 2, we compared NPMI and MTA for different ways to combine comparable EuroNews articles in tuples. For Decision 3, models trained with different numbers of training document tuples were contrasted. Comparisons were made on the basis of linear regressions, and visual inspections. These assessments then guided the selection of the one model that we used to infer topics for the REMINDER corpus. The selected model was labeled first individually by five researchers fluent in EN, DE, and ES. The final labels were defined following a joint discussion amongst researchers.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: When contrasting different data types for tuple creation (Decision 1), all created models were used (N = 310). Please see the regression results in Table A4, Online Appendix. In terms of NPMI, models based on synthetic parallel data were evaluated significantly worse than models based on EuroNews articles. For MTA the opposite pattern was observed. Here, models based on synthetic parallel data performed significantly better than models based on EuroNews articles. This means that tuples created based on EuroNews articles resulted in more coherent topics and tuples created with directly translated documents (synthetic parallel data) to more consistent topics across languages.

For the comparison of alignment strategies (Decision 2), only models trained with 250 EuroNews tuples were used. Please see the regression results Table A4, Online Appendix. In terms of NMPI, models trained with EuroNews tuples aligned merely via publication date performed significantly worse than EuroNews tuples for which LOC, ORG, or PER were used as additional information on top of the publication date. Aligning EuroNews articles with named entities in addition to the publication date led also to significantly better MTA performance compared to the simpler strategy to merely align articles via publication date. As the standardized regression coefficients indicate, this was equally and on very similar levels overserved for additional alignment via LOC, ORG, and PER. Thus, the strategy to align news articles based on a shared publication date and shared named entity led to more coherent and to more consistent topics.

Turning to Decision 3, the guiding rationale was to translate only as many articles as needed in order to reduce costs. To learn about the impact of different numbers of tuples created, we compared the coherence and consistency of all models that are based on synthetic parallel data (N = 240). Two linear regressions for which NMPI and MTA were used as DVs and K as control show that both evaluation metrics increased significantly as the number of training tuples increased. Figure 2 shows that NPMI and MTA rose particularly strongly up to about 300 tuples and steadily but more slowly thereafter.

We selected one model that we used to infer topic distributions θ for all original language articles of the REMINDER corpus. Choosing between a model based on synthetic parallel data and one based on non-parallel but comparable data, we looked for a model with the best possible compromise between high NPMI and MTA value. A model that had higher NPMI and MTA values compared to the median NMPI (M = .09) and median MTA (M = 20.75) of all models. This criterion was fulfilled by 114 out of the 310 models. All of these 114 were based on synthetic parallel data. Given that our analysis for decision 3 showed that lower numbers of training tuples resulted in acceptable yet a higher number (if available) to even better model evaluation metrics, we decided to select one that was trained with the maximum of created tuples, here 5,000. The selected model has a NPMI of 0.11 and a MTA of 23.75. The model output consisted of 20 multilingual topics -each is divided in three wordlists that represent the topic's EN, DE, and ES. The manually created labels for the 20 multilingual topics and the top 10 words per language are shown in Table 1.

sections.header_raw: Evaluating the validity of the final model
sections.header_norm: evaluating the validity of the final model
paragraphs: To evaluate the validity of the selected model (Table 1), we used it to infer topic distributions for the full REMINDER corpus. Figure 3 shows the relative topic salience across countries and over time. Figure 3 can help to get a first impression about whether expectations regarding the salience of individual topics in the different countries and at certain points in time are mirrored in the topic visualization. Such expectations can arise from the topic words and matching topic labels and be based on the case-specific knowledge, results from other studies, or on real-world events. For example, Figure 3 shows that topic 9 "EU politics" became more salient in the UK than in Germany and Spain starting around 2016, which is the year of the UK-wide referendum about leaving or remaining in the EU. Topic 7 'Refugee accommodation" with topic words like "asylum seeker" and "accommodation" is much more salient in Germany than in Spain or the UK (Figure 3); an observation that can be related to an empirical result by Heidenreich et al. (2019) who found for the time period between Jan 2015 and Dec 2019, that the accommodation topic was relatively strong in German media coverage compared to the Hungarian, the UK, Spanish, or Swedish coverage. We conclude from the correspondence between expectations and the observed lines that the PLTM model can be considered to have some face validity.

We could further test convergent validity for the selected model through a comparison of the topic probabilities per document with an external trusted variable for the same documents (e.g., Guo et al., 2016). Lacking manual codings available for these documents, we decided to make use of keyword-based dictionaries created for English, German, and Spanish. We used dictionaries designed to measure an economy & budget, a security, and a welfare frame in migration-related news articles. We provide more information about the dictionaries, their definition, creation and validation by native speakers in Online Appendix A3. The three binomial poisson models that we run to assess the relatedness between the topic probabilities for the 20 identified topics and the three dictionary annotated frames are shown in Online Appendix Table A8. The number of security keywords (identified by the security dictionary) is most strongly related to the topic probabilities of topic 4 labeled "Security." The number of economy keywords per document (identified by the economy & budget dictionary) is most strongly related to the topic probabilities of topic 10 labeled "Economy." The result is less unequivocal for the welfare dictionary. We find especially strong relationships for Topic 2 "Education" and 19 "Family." Considering the definition of the welfare dictionary (see Online Appendix, Table A5), which explicitly includes references to education as well as child and family support, this finding seems conclusive overall.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: Our application of PLTM has provided grounds for considering it a promising approach for multilingual comparative research. Contextualizing our results, we now move on to contrast PLTM again with other strategies to assess scenarios that justify the effort of implementing it. PLTM first of all appeals because it has none of the following downsides of the earlier presented strategies for comparative research (here named strategy A, B, and C). Strategy A, monolingual corpus selection, is very much limited by its dependency on such monolingual but cross-case or international text resources and the related restrictions for the research questions and the generalizability of results. PLTM does not set such bounds. As long as training documents can be found or created, it can be used for various languages. When relying on strategy B, running one topic model per language/case, qualitative comparisons between case-specific topics is possible and may may be adequate for certain goals and a certain approach to comparability, but as Heidenreich et al. (2019) note, "direct comparability of topics across countries is limited, as some topics may be similar but not the same" (p. 8) across languages. Such a strategy "will preclude any numerical comparisons of scores across cultures." (He & van de Vijver, 2012, p. 4). The PLTM, in contrast, allows direct comparison across countries and, for example, the plotting of saliences in the same graph (see Figure 3).

Disadvantages of the three techniques presented with strategy C retain the costs (and quality for some languages) of proper machine translation (Lucas et al., 2015), the troublesome creation and validation of multilingual dictionaries (Maier et al., 2020), or the rather laborious interpretation of the cross-lingual topics as the topics are not represented by keywords (Chan et al., 2020). With PLTM, in 19. Family 20. Mediterranean route 16. Law 17. Migration as crisis 18. Sports 13. Media & journalism 14. History & books 15. Housing 10. Economy 11. Culture (art & music) 12. Food 7. Refugee accommodation 8. Democracy & values 9. EU politics 4. Security 5. Culture (film & theatre) 6.War 1. Welfare & jobs 2. Education 3. Election 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 -0.2 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 Relative topic salience country Germany Spain UK Relative topic salience over time and across countries comparison, we can't do without machine translation either, but at least with considerably less. Furthermore, no multilingual dictionaries are necessary, and the model output (see Table 1) is close to the format of LDA or STM. Similar to the three strategies, neither PLTM nor MuPTM-s more generally offer the perfect solution. Lucas et al. (2015) provide reasons for why these models have not yet gained a foothold in the social sciences. There is the imperfection of the lexical resources, the (in-)comparability of the training tuples (model input), the validation of the model output, the lack of publicly available and easy to use software implementations, and not at least the inability to include document level covariates. To elaborate briefly on each, the imperfection of lexical resources is the main limitation of the first type of MuPTM-s explained on page 13. Bilingual dictionaries will never include all possible translations, will always only consider words that have a counterpart in another language, may miss more technical vocabulary, and may not be available for low-resource languages. The second critical point, the "comparability of the training tuples" concerns the second type of MuPTM-s such as PLTM. It is true that the training tuples already need to be "relatively well annotated and understood" (Boyd-Graber & Blei, 2009, p. 75). Therefore, with models like PLTM the big advantage of clustering unknown text documents using unsupervised methods cannot be fully exploited. In fact, PLTM is no longer unsupervised. It is not completely inductive and data-driven as it requires training documents, and may be influenced by the training procedure and related decisions. Obtaining and preparing the training documents can be resource-intensive. Still, by using synthetic parallel data or aligned EuroNews articles, we have shown techniques that are suitable for creating comparable training tuples. It is important to note that the domain and thus vocabulary of the training tuples should be as similar as possible to the corpus being modeled (here REMINDER corpus). We addressed the next critical point, "the user needs to verify that the topic word distributions are comparable across languages " (Lucas et al., 2015, p. 262) with the MTA metric. Still, it has to be noted that PLTM requires some language skills of human coders for the labeling and interpretation of the model output. While it seems that giving up on document-level covariates is currently a prerequisite, the fact that there is one public software implementation for PLTM is, in fact, a great opportunity for the application of the PLTM. By providing instructions and code for R and Python users, we seek to facilitate its implementation and make it even more accessible among communication science researchers. Ending this section with an advantage of PLTM, we like to draw attention to the model output (e.g., Table 1). With PLTM's focus on linguistic details, it is possible to identify differences and similarities in topic emphasis between languages. The ability to allow for close inspection of linguistic peculiarities is a unique selling point of PLTM compared to the other strategies presented.

A direct empirical comparison between the model outputs of PLTM and the three techniques summarized as strategy C was, however, beyond our resources and the scope of this paper. Future studies could, for example, implement a validation method by Chan et al. (2020) where the document topic probabilities derived from each strategy or technique are contrasted via a comparison with a manually coded ground truth. This way, future studies could assess empirically when and under what circumstances each strategy will perform best.

sections.paper_id: Lind et al. - 2022 - Building the Bridge Topic Modeling for Comparative Research
paragraphs: The aim of this study was to present PLTM as one example for a MuPTM that can help to facilitate multilingual topic modeling endeavors for comparative communication research. By demonstrating the usability of PLTM for one case we have made a first step in this direction. By covering the aspects, type of corpus, research goal, comparability approach (emic/etic), availability of resources, we have provided a basis for deciding in which scenario PLTM is a good choice (see Figure 1). Methodologically, we provided guidance for the selection of training documents and showed techniques to automatically evaluate the coherence and consistency of multilingual topics. While both "synthetic parallel training documents" and "non-parallel but topically comparable training documents" led to somewhat coherent and consistent multilingual topics, if given the choice and the financial means to translate a part of the full corpus, synthetic parallel data may be preferred. Not least because it saves the procurement or creation of another training document corpus, but also because one remains particularly close to the vocabulary of the text corpus to be modeled. Still, training PLTMs with self-created "non-parallel but topically comparable training documents" was also successful. Creating EuroNews tuples via the publication date in combination with shared named entities appeared to be a useful technique. While the model evaluation values could be increased with a larger number of training documents, even smaller quantities lead to decent results. Both the NPMI and MTA matched up with manual checks and can be recommended as automated metrics for the evaluation of the PLTM model output. Following our results and especially the methodological experiments by Pruss et al. (2019), we encourage communication scientists to further test the application and methodological boundaries of PLTM in their own research.
